operating_state: Tesla achieved $81.5 billion in total revenue for 2022, representing 51% growth driven by record vehicle deliveries exceeding 1.3 million units globally. Production capacity expanded significantly with Gigafactory Berlin-Brandenburg and Gigafactory Texas commencing operations while Shanghai maintained strong output despite temporary COVID-related shutdowns. Energy generation and storage revenue increased 40% driven by higher deployments, while services revenue grew 60% from increased used vehicle sales and paid Supercharging. Tesla Semi entered early production and deliveries in December 2022.
cost_structure: Automotive gross margin improved to 19.3% driven by manufacturing efficiencies and reduced per-unit costs, though total automotive gross margin compressed from 29.3% to 28.5% as rising raw material, logistics, and warranty costs offset production gains. Operating expenses increased primarily due to workforce expansion to 127,855 employees globally while administrative expenses decreased as percentage of revenue from 8% to 5% primarily due to reduced stock-based compensation expense. Raw material cost inflation for aluminum, steel, cobalt, lithium, nickel and copper pressured margins, partially offset by long-term supply contracts and design optimizations.
material_events: Tesla completed a three-for-one stock split in August 2022 and recorded bitcoin impairment losses while realizing gains from digital asset conversions. The company recognized revenue from general FSD feature release in North America and increased regulatory credit revenue. Capital expenditures reached $7.2 billion primarily for expansions at Texas, Berlin-Brandenburg, and Shanghai facilities. Tesla launched insurance products using real-time driving behavior data and established a new unsecured revolving credit facility.
macro_exposures: Foreign currency exposures intensified with potential impact from adverse exchange rate movements, primarily affecting Chinese yuan, euro, and British pound positions with limited hedging activities. Global semiconductor shortages continued impacting supply chain and production schedules while COVID-19 variants caused temporary shutdowns at Shanghai and supplier facilities. Rising interest rates pose demand risk as consumers may reduce discretionary spending on vehicles, while supply chain vulnerabilities include dependence on specialized raw materials for battery production amid increasing global electric vehicle competition.
forward_outlook: Management projects capital expenditures of $6.0-8.0 billion in 2023 and $7.0-9.0 billion annually for the following two years to support manufacturing expansion and new product development. Strategic focus remains on vertical integration of battery cell production and supply chain localization to reduce dependency on external suppliers while expanding insurance product offerings and developing autonomous Tesla ride-hailing network leveraging Full Self-Driving technology.
uncertainty_profile: Tesla faces material legal uncertainties including pending race discrimination litigation, ongoing DOJ investigation into Autopilot features, and multiple derivative lawsuits alleging breach of fiduciary duty. Production ramp execution at new Berlin-Brandenburg and Texas facilities faces inherent manufacturing startup risks including regulatory compliance and workforce development. Automotive regulatory credit revenue remains subject to evolving government policies while self-driving vehicle regulations continue developing globally with potential restrictions on advanced driver assistance features.
